---
title: "Part VII. Advanced Topics"
---

::: {.part-hero}
## Part VII

This part covers the mature edges of Bayesian workflow: model uncertainty, robustification, design under uncertainty, communication, and capstone-level synthesis.
:::

## Part Overview

Part VII extends the core workflow into the topics that distinguish mature Bayesian practice from elementary posterior calculation: model uncertainty, robustness, experimental design, communication, and open-ended projects.

## Part Learning Goals

By the end of Part VII, learners should be able to:

- compare models using predictive and marginal-likelihood criteria with appropriate caution;
- formulate robust alternatives under misspecification and outliers;
- reason about design under uncertainty and utility;
- communicate Bayesian results responsibly to scientific collaborators.

## Fully Written Chapters In This Repository

- [Model selection and averaging](../chapters/advanced/model-selection-and-averaging.qmd)
- [Robust Bayesian methods](../chapters/advanced/robust-bayesian-methods.qmd)
- [Bayesian experimental design](../chapters/advanced/bayesian-experimental-design.qmd)
- [Communication and interpretation](../chapters/advanced/communication-and-interpretation.qmd)
- [Capstone projects](../chapters/advanced/capstone-projects.qmd)

## Reading Path

Recommended order:

1. [Model selection and averaging](../chapters/advanced/model-selection-and-averaging.qmd)
2. [Robust Bayesian methods](../chapters/advanced/robust-bayesian-methods.qmd)
3. [Bayesian experimental design](../chapters/advanced/bayesian-experimental-design.qmd)
4. [Communication and interpretation](../chapters/advanced/communication-and-interpretation.qmd)
5. [Capstone projects](../chapters/advanced/capstone-projects.qmd)

## Chapter 27. Model Selection and Averaging

### Core question

How should we compare or combine models when model uncertainty itself matters?

### Section blueprint

- Bayes factors and marginal likelihood.
- Bridge sampling.
- Bayesian model averaging.
- Stacking.
- Projection predictive selection.

### Derivations that must appear

- Marginal likelihood identity.
- Posterior model probabilities under prior model weights.
- Predictive-risk intuition for stacking.

### Computational lab

- Estimate a marginal likelihood with bridge sampling.
- Compare PSIS-LOO model ranking with Bayes factors under prior sensitivity.

### Common pitfalls

- Using Bayes factors with vague or improper priors.
- Confusing explanatory and predictive goals in model comparison.
- Treating one selection method as universally optimal.

### Exercises

- Show how posterior model probabilities depend on prior model probabilities.
- Compare BMA and stacking conceptually on a misspecified model set.

### Core references

- @robert2007
- @vehtari2017

### Full chapter

- [Model selection and averaging](../chapters/advanced/model-selection-and-averaging.qmd)

## Chapter 28. Robust Bayesian Methods

### Core question

How can Bayesian models remain useful when data contain outliers or the likelihood is misspecified?

### Section blueprint

- Heavy-tailed priors.
- Heavy-tailed likelihoods.
- Contamination models.
- Outlier indicators and latent-mixture robustness.
- Misspecification and posterior concentration issues.

### Derivations that must appear

- Student-$t$ likelihood as a scale-mixture intuition.
- Contamination-model factorization.
- Influence of heavy-tailed priors on posterior shrinkage.

### Computational lab

- Compare Gaussian and Student-$t$ regression under outliers.
- Fit a contamination mixture and inspect posterior outlier probabilities.

### Common pitfalls

- Overusing robustness as a substitute for understanding the data-generating process.
- Ignoring the computational costs of mixture-based robustification.

### Exercises

- Show why a Student-$t$ model downweights outliers more than a Gaussian model.
- Compare posterior predictive behavior under robust and non-robust likelihoods.

### Core references

- @gelman2013
- @murphy2023

### Full chapter

- [Robust Bayesian methods](../chapters/advanced/robust-bayesian-methods.qmd)

## Chapter 29. Bayesian Experimental Design

### Core question

How should one choose data collection strategies when uncertainty and utility are both explicit?

### Section blueprint

- Utility-based design.
- Expected information gain.
- Sequential and adaptive design.
- Active learning overview.
- Computational challenges in design optimization.

### Derivations that must appear

- Expected utility criterion for design selection.
- Mutual-information intuition for information gain.
- Posterior update within a sequential design loop.

### Computational lab

- Compare fixed and adaptive sampling in a toy binary-outcome experiment.
- Approximate design utility by Monte Carlo.

### Common pitfalls

- Confusing parameter uncertainty reduction with decision utility.
- Ignoring design-stage prior sensitivity.

### Exercises

- Write a Monte Carlo estimator for expected utility of a design.
- Compare two candidate designs under different utility functions.

### Core references

- @berger1985
- @mackay2003

### Full chapter

- [Bayesian experimental design](../chapters/advanced/bayesian-experimental-design.qmd)

## Chapter 30. Communication and Interpretation

### Core question

How should Bayesian conclusions be reported so that uncertainty, model assumptions, and decision relevance are all clear?

### Section blueprint

- Reporting posterior summaries.
- Interval interpretation.
- Effect sizes and uncertainty communication.
- Decision-oriented summaries.
- Visual communication.
- Reproducibility and literate analysis.

### Derivations and conceptual targets

- Translate posterior distributions into scientifically meaningful contrasts.
- Explain posterior probability statements without frequency misuse.
- Distinguish predictive uncertainty from parameter uncertainty in communication.

### Computational lab

- Build a one-page results summary from posterior draws.
- Compare multiple interval types and visual summaries.

### Common pitfalls

- Reporting only posterior means.
- Hiding assumptions behind polished plots.
- Interpreting high posterior probability as practical importance.

### Exercises

- Rewrite a technical posterior summary for a nontechnical collaborator.
- Build a plot that separates posterior uncertainty from observed-data noise.

### Core references

- @gabry2019
- @gelman2013

### Full chapter

- [Communication and interpretation](../chapters/advanced/communication-and-interpretation.qmd)

## Chapter 31. Capstone Projects

### Core question

How do all components of Bayesian workflow combine in a real, open-ended project?

### Section blueprint

- Project scoping and question formulation.
- Data auditing and exploratory analysis.
- Prior design and model specification.
- Fitting, diagnostics, and criticism.
- Sensitivity analysis.
- Final communication.

### Required outputs

- reproducible code;
- prior predictive checks;
- posterior diagnostics;
- predictive evaluation;
- sensitivity analysis;
- written discussion of limitations.

### Computational lab

- Use [Projects](../projects.qmd) as the canonical project bank.

### Common pitfalls

- Starting with a complicated model before articulating the scientific question.
- Underreporting computational failures.
- Omitting sensitivity analysis.

### Exercises

- Draft a one-page project proposal with model family, prior strategy, diagnostics plan, and evaluation criteria.

### Full chapter

- [Capstone projects](../chapters/advanced/capstone-projects.qmd)
